text:
Networked control systems find practical utility in diverse
2
v applications such as distributed optimization, power sharing
0 in microgrids, clock synchronization in sensor networks, and
4 robotic and aerospace multi-agent systems. This paper focuses
4
on one of their significant security concerns, the zero dynamics
8
attacks (ZDAs) [1]–[6], which discreetly embed attack signals
1
. in  the null  space of  control  systems  to alter  the system
7
behaviors maliciously. These attacks  inherently cloak their
0
impact by ensuring that  the modified system output mirrors
4
2 its unaltered counterpart,  thereby posing great  risks  to the
:
systems’ stability, operation, and sustainability.
v
i  For  linear  systems, ZDAs can be classified mainly into
X three types: intrinsic ZDAs [1]–[3], sampling ZDAs [4], and
r
enforced ZDAs [5], [6], which exploit specific characteristics
a
of  systems  such as  invariant  zeros,  discretization-induced
unstable zeros, and system redundancies, respectively. All of
these attacks can make the systems’ states diverge completely
stealthily, even if the systems are both controllable and ob-
servable. Their robust counterpart [7] further extends this idea
to construct disruptive attacks for nonlinear systems and linear
∗ Department of Aerospace Engineering, University of Illinois at Urbana-
Champaign, Urbana, IL, hiroyasu@illinois.edu.
† Division of Engineering and Applied Science, Caltech, Pasadena, CA,
{jdibrahi, jhajar, jragan, sjchung, hadaegh}@caltech.edu.
‡  Jet  Propulsion Laboratory,  Pasadena, CA  hiroyasu.tsukamoto,
{
fred.y.hadaegh}@jpl.nasa.gov.
This research is funded by the Technology Innovation Institution (TII)
under a contract with Caltech. The first author is funded by the University
of Illinois at Urbana-Champaign. Part of the research was carried out at the Jet
Propulsion Laboratory, California Institute of Technology, under a contract with
the National Aeronautics and Space Administration.
© 2024 IEEE. Personal use of this material is permitted. Permission from IEE
reprinting/republishing this material for advertising or promotional purposes,
reuse of any copyrighted component of this work in other w

1
Topology Switching for
ics Attacks
, James Ragan†, Soon-Jo Chung†, Fred Y. Hadaegh†‡
systems with uncertainty. It is also worth noting that the ZDAs
have a tight connection to the zero dynamics and internal
dynamics of [8], which implies a great threat also in robotics
and aerospace systems  (e.g.,  hybrid robotics  [9], PVTOL
aircrafts [10], bipedal/quadrupedal robots [9], UAVs [11], [12],
and robotic manipulators [13]).
Contributions:  Inheriting  the performance and security
metrics of existing literature (see,  e.g.,  the controllability,
observability, robustness, and sensitivity metrics of [?], [14]–
[16] and the references therein), we formulate our optimization
problem for robust optimal detection and mitigation of  the
three  types of ZDAs,  explicitly expressed in terms of  the
networked linear control  systems’ communication topology.
Our main contribution is the reformulation of  this problem
into a mathematically equivalent rank-constrained optimization
problem with the topology being a decision variable. This class
of problems can be solved computationally efficiently with
convex rank-minimization techniques [17]–[21]. We perform
numerical simulations to demonstrate the effectiveness of our
proposed robust optimal  topology switching strategy for a
network of double integrators. The results highlight how our
method robustly and proactively averts ZDAs while keeping
systems controllable and observable.
Related Work: Numerous studies have focused on improving
the reliability and security of networked control systems. [1],
[22]  introduce security metrics that assess both the impact
and detectability of attacks, informing the design of resilient
observer-based controllers and detectors against stealthy at-
tacks. [?], [16] are another fundamental works that establish
a theoretical  foundation for robustly monitoring and detect-
ing attacks in cyber-secure networked systems. As discussed
extensively in these studies, a unique property of ZDAs is
that  they are not detectable through the systems’ output by
definition. This  implies  that  the only counterstrategy is  to
change the dynamics somehow,  e.g., using the generalized
hold [4]. Topology-switching strategies for ZDA detection are
also based on this idea as proposed in [2]. [11] further delves
into defense tactics against novel ZDA-based stealthy attacks,
where defenders switch between crafted network topologies
to detect ZDAs white attackers simultaneously adapt  to the
dependers’ strategy.

As an example of practical applications,
[12] considers ZDAs and covert attacks on networked UAVs
in formation control, presenting decentralized and centralized
detection schemes that leverage the communication topology
switching of UAVs.
𝑛×𝑛
Notations: For 𝐴 ∈ R  , we use 𝐴 ≻ 0, 𝐴 ⪰ 0, 𝐴 ≺ 0, and
𝐴 ⪯ 0 for the positive definite, positive semi-definite, negative
definite, negative semi-definite matrices, respectively. For 𝑥 ∈
E must be obtained for all other uses, in any current or future media, including
creating new collective works, for resale or redistribution to servers or lists, or
orks. Digital Object Identifier (DOI): TO BE ASSIGNED..

metadata.section_title: Preliminaries: Zero Dynamics Attacks and Problem Statement

text:
We consider the following networked linear control system:
𝑥(cid:164)(𝑡) = 𝐴(𝑡)𝑥(𝑡) + 𝐵(𝑡, 𝜈(𝑡)) (𝑢(𝑡, 𝑦(𝑡), 𝜈(𝑡)) +𝑎(𝑡))  (1a)
𝑦(𝑡) = 𝐶(𝑡, 𝜈(𝑡))𝑥(𝑡)  (1b)
where 𝑡 ∈ R+, 𝜈 : R+ ↦→T is the topology representation of the
network (see Assumption 2) with T being the set of topologies,
𝑝  𝑟  𝑞
𝑥 : R+ ↦→R  is the stacked system state, 𝑢 : R+ ×R ×T ↦→R
𝑞
is  the stacked system control  input,  𝑎 : R+ ↦→ R  is  the
𝑟
stacked attack control input, 𝑦 : R+ ↦→R  is the stacked system
𝑝×𝑝  𝑝×𝑞
measurement, and 𝐴 : R+ ↦→ R  , 𝐵 : R+ ×T ↦→ R  , and
𝑟×𝑝
𝐶 : R+ ×T ↦→R  are the stacked system matrices.
Remark 1.  This  formulation can be viewed as a general
closed-loop dynamical system, where 𝑥 consists of the actual
state, estimated state for feedback control, and estimated state
for anomaly detection as in [23].
In networked control systems in robotics, aerospace, and
cyber-physical systems, switching the network topology 𝜈 does
not change the open-loop systemmatrix 𝐴 of the system (1a) as
it is often determined by the underlying dynamics. The closed-
loop 𝐴 can be modified by the feedback control 𝑢.
Assumption 1. We assume the following in (1).
(a) The functions 𝐴, 𝐵, and 𝐶 are known both to the defender
and the attacker.
(b) The defender cannot directly measure the attack 𝑎.
(c) The defender can switch the network topology for all 𝑡,
and the attacker also knows the topology.
(d) The actuation and sensing are performed discretely with
the zero-order hold (see, e.g., [24]), with Δ𝑡𝑢 and Δ𝑡𝑦
being the control actuation and sensing sampling period,
respectively.
(e) The network topology 𝜈(𝑡) is fixed during 𝑡 ∈ [𝑘Δ𝑡𝑦, (𝑘 +
1)Δ𝑡𝑦], ∀𝑘 ∈ N.
𝑁×𝑁
Assumption 2  (Network Topology).  Let Θ𝑘 ∈ {0, 1}
denote the adjacency matrix of  the network at each sensing
time step 𝑘 of (e) in Assumption 1, where 𝑁 is the number of
𝑁×𝑁
agents and {0, 1}  is the set of 𝑁 by 𝑁 matrices with their
entries restricted to 0 or 1. The network topology 𝜈 of (1) is
represented by Θ𝑘, which describes the communication and
physical connections of the network at each time step 𝑘. Also,
the adjacency matrix Θ𝑘  lies in a time-varying, finite set of
matrices T𝑘 ⊂ T that represents all  the possible distributed
communications and physical connections of the network.
Assumption 2 is mainly used for expressing the attack
detection and attenuation problem in terms of  the network
topology 𝜈 in Sec. IV. This is also useful for dealing withmobile
agents with time-varying T𝑘 due to distributed communications,
as to be demonstrated in Sec. V.

ACCEPTED, IEEE CONFERENCE ON DECISION AND CONTROL (CDC), 2024.
A. Zero Dynamics Attack (ZDA)
The ZDA is a stealthy attack designed based on the assump-
tions (a) and (b) of Assumption 1. In this paper, we define a
stealthy (or undetectable [16]) attack as follows.
Definition 1. For a given terminal  time 𝑡𝐹, an attack 𝑎(𝑡)
of (1a) is stealthy in 𝑡 ∈ [0, 𝑡𝐹] if, for some initial states, we
have 𝑦(𝑡) = 𝑦𝑛(𝑡) for ∀𝑡 ∈ [0, 𝑡𝐹], where 𝑦 is given by (1b) and
𝑦𝑛 is the nominal unattacked output ((1b) with 𝑎 = 0).
We focus on the ZDA as one of the most common stealthy
attacks. It works for a wide range of real-world systems (e.g.,
robotics, aerospace, and cyber-physical systems) even when they
are both controllable and observable. Note that although our
method deals with the ZDAs for linear time-varying systems, as
will be seen in the following sections, some of the definitions
below are given for linear time-invariant systems just to provide
readers with high-level ideas of the ZDAs.
1)  Intrinsic ZDA: This is a stealthy attack based on the
invariant zeros 𝑧inv ∈ C of the system (1), at which the following
equation with the Rosenbrock system matrix
(cid:20)𝑧  I  𝐴  𝐵(cid:21) (cid:20)𝑥  (cid:21)
inv −  −  𝑎0
= 0  (2)
𝐶  O  𝑢𝑎0
gives a nontrivial solution  𝑥⊤ 𝑢⊤ ⊤  0, assuming that the
[ 𝑎0  𝑎0]  ≠
system matrices are time-invariant and the network topology 𝜈
is fixed [1], [3]. The intrinsic ZDA has a tight connection to the
zero dynamics and internal dynamics of [8] that often appear
in hybrid robotics [9]. In fact, for linear systems, the system
eigenvalues of the zero dynamics are equal to the invariant zeros
of (2) [25, pp. 297-299].
Lemma 1.  If there exist 𝑧inv, 𝑥𝑎0, 𝑢𝑎0 that satisfy (2) for a
𝑧inv𝑡
fixed network topology, an attack constructed as 𝑎 = 𝑒  𝑢𝑎0
with 𝑥(0) = 𝑥𝑛0 + 𝑥𝑎0 is stealthy in the sense of Definition 1,
where 𝑥𝑛0 is a nominal initial state for (1) with 𝑎 = 0.
Proof. Let  𝑋(𝑠) = L[𝑥(𝑡)], 𝑈(𝑠) = L[𝑢(𝑡)],  and 𝑌 (𝑠) =
L[𝑦(𝑡)]  for  the Laplace transform L. Since we have (𝑠 −

𝑧inv)  (𝑧invI− 𝐴) = −I + (𝑠 − 𝑧inv)  (𝑠I− 𝐴) for any 𝑠, applying
L to (1a) with the first row of (2) yields

The second row of (2) with (1b)  then gives 𝑌 (𝑠) = 𝐶(𝑠I −

metadata.section_title: Preliminaries: Zero Dynamics Attacks and Problem Statement

text:
□
Lemma 1 indicates that the intrinsic ZDA stealthily makes
𝑥 of  (1a) diverge if  the system is non-minimum-phase ⇔
Re(𝑧inv) > 0 ⇔ the zero dynamics are unstable (see [1], [2]
for discrete-time systems and [11],  [16] for continuous-time
systems for more details). The zeros 𝑧inv are “invariant” under
coordinate changes and state feedback, which represent one
of  the “intrinsic” properties of zero dynamics and internal
dynamics [26, pp. 218 -228].
Example 1. The linearized system of the cart-pole balancing
with the position measurement is subject to the ZDA, although
it  is both controllable and observable. This  is part of  the
reason why the ZDA can be a great  threat  in robotics [9]
(see Fig. 1). Some other examples include, but are not limited.

In order  to quantify the quality of  the network topology
switching as in Example 2,  this section defines several per-
formance metrics expressed in terms of the network topology 𝜈
of (1). Most of the results here are preliminaries but explicitly
stated here just to make this paper as self-contained as possible.
Those familiar with the topics to be covered here could skip this
section and proceed to our main contribution in Section IV.
We consider finite-horizon metrics so we can handle time-
varying ZDA detection and mitigation strategies.
Remark 2.  In the subsequent discussion, we suppose that
Assumption 1 holds unless stated otherwise. Also, we let ℓ
and 𝑘 be the sampling indices for the control and sensing,
respectively,  𝑡𝐹 be the terminal  time, 𝑥𝑆  is the initial state,
𝑥𝐹 is the terminal state, 𝑡ℓ = ℓΔ𝑡𝑢 and 𝑡𝑘 = 𝑘Δ𝑡𝑦 for the time
periods of (d), 𝐿 = ⌊𝑡𝐹/Δ𝑡𝑢⌋ and 𝐾 = ⌊𝑡𝐹/Δ𝑡𝑦⌋ for the floor
function ⌊·⌋, and 𝑢ℓ = 𝑢(𝑡ℓ , 𝑦(𝑡ℓ), 𝜈(𝑡ℓ)), 𝑎ℓ = 𝑎(𝑡ℓ), 𝑥𝑘 = 𝑥(𝑡𝑘),
𝐴𝑘 = 𝐴(𝑡𝑘), 𝐵𝑘 = 𝐵(𝑡𝑘, 𝜈(𝑡𝑘)), and 𝐶𝑘 = 𝐶(𝑡𝑘, 𝜈(𝑡𝑘))  in the
system (1).

C. Transient Attack Robustness Metric
This metric can be defined with the following transient H∞

D. Transient Minimum Attack Sensitivity Metric
This is the most important metric for the detection of the

metadata.section_title: Main Contribution: Robust and Optimal Network Topology Switching

text:
Now that we have metrics for quantifying the key system
performances, this section presents several numerically efficient
optimization approaches for designing switching strategies of
the network topology. We still suppose that Assumption 1 holds,
and we use the notation of Remark 2.
A. Controlling Network Topology
Feedbacking the network output as follows provides addi-
tional control authority over  the modification of  the system
matrices:

B. Convex Formulation
Controllability, observability, and non-stealthiness (given in
Definition 1) can be guaranteed as long as the controllability,
observability, and minimum attack sensitivity metrics, respec-
tively, are positive, and thus they are best used as constraints.
Also, controllability, observability, and non-stealthiness are in-
variant under feedback, the third of which is shown in Lemma 9.
The optimization problem for minimizing the robustness metric
of Lemma 6 under  these constraints can be formulated as
follows:

This section demonstrates the robust and optimal network
topology switching of Sec. V for networked double integrators.
As seen in Example 2, this network is subject to ZDAs even
when the system is both controllable and observable.

This paper proposes a novel  framework for  robust  and
optimal topology switching in networked linear control systems,

8
specifically addressing intrinsic, sampling, and enforced ZDAs.
We utilize a performance metric explicitly expressed in terms
of the network communication topology, which aims to min-
imize the classical H∞ norm while capturing controllability,
observability, and attack sensitivity via constraints. The key
contribution of our method is the transformation of the metric
minimization problem into an equivalent rank-constrained opti-
mization problem, offering an efficient convex optimization-
based approach  to  implementing  robust  optimal  topology
switching. Our simulations of networked linear systems show
that optimal topology switching can robustly and optimally de-
tect andmitigate ZDAs whilemaintaining systemcontrollability
and observability. Future work would include extensions of our
framework to nonlinear systems with uncertainty, consideration
of sensor attacks, and incorporation of learning and data-driven
methods for evolving stealth attack patterns.
